cards.back: A computational system that performs tasks which would require human intelligence if a person did them — e.g. perception, language, reasoning, or decision-making.
AI built and trained to do one specific task, with no ability to transfer that competence to a different task. Essentially all deployed AI today is narrow.
A hypothetical AI with flexible, human-like intelligence able to learn or perform *any* intellectual task, transferring knowledge across domains. Not yet achieved by any real system.
AI whose behaviour is defined entirely by explicit, hand-written rules or logic (e.g. `if`/`then` statements) rather than learned from data.
AI where a system automatically adjusts its own parameters to improve at a task by training on example data, instead of following hand-written rules.
A classic rule-based AI application that encodes a human expert's knowledge as a large set of if-then rules to give advice or diagnoses in one narrow domain (e.g. MYCIN for infection diagnosis).
The set of example inputs (often paired with correct outputs) used by a learning algorithm to adjust a system's parameters.
Alan Turing's 1950 proposal: a machine can be considered to behave intelligently if a human judge, conversing by text with both a human and the machine, cannot reliably tell which is which.
The tendency for people to read genuine understanding into a program's output even when it is produced by simple rule-based pattern matching, named after the 1960s chatbot ELIZA.
A description of a system (typically learning-based) whose internal decision-making is difficult for a human to inspect or explain, even though its inputs and outputs are observable.
